coredata.dc:title: U(VI) capture from water-based systems by decorated nanohybrid of Zn-BTC MOF with GQDs-rGO and alginate hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Tharwat, Rana M.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
Hassan, Saad S.M.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, zinc-based MOF utilizing trimesic acid (Zn-BTC) was combined with carbon-based materials, viz. graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and all were crosslinked with alginate hydrogel to finally generate Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel as a novel nanohybrid composite with various incorporated oxygen functionalities. The well-formed beads of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite were employed to favor adsorptive capture and uptake of U(VI) from water due to the strong binding affinity of U(VI) ions to the loaded oxygen functional groups in the assembled composite. Multiple active functionalities, crystalline structure and average particle size (19.43 nm) were characterized. The EDX analysis referred to C: 47.89%, O: 44.77%, Zn: 0.43%, Ca: 2.82%, Cl: 1.74%, and Na: 2.23%. U(VI) adsorptive uptake was based on endothermic and spontaneous. Linear-nonlinear kinetic expressions confirmed high fitted to pseudo-second order kinetics with R2 0.99 and 0.94 for linear and nonlinear, respectively, while, the good agreement between the computed qe = 36.76 mg/g and qexp = 39.01 mg/g referred to the best fitting process by this model. The adsorptive uptake isotherms of U(VI) denoted mostly to good fitting with Langmuir adsorption isotherm in both linear and nonlinear representations. Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel as a novel nanohybrid composite was successfully implemented in removal of U(VI) from seawater, tap water and wastewater providing uptake percentages 93.52%, 90.51% and 92.68%, respectively. The evaluated composite was recycled 5 times and exhibited excellent effectiveness and high stability in U(VI) uptake with higher uptake capability when compared to other adsorbents.

head.ce:title: U(VI) capture from water-based systems by decorated nanohybrid of Zn-BTC MOF with GQDs-rGO and alginate hydrogel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, zinc-based MOF utilizing trimesic acid (Zn-BTC) was combined with carbon-based materials, viz. graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and all were crosslinked with alginate hydrogel to finally generate Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel as a novel nanohybrid composite with various incorporated oxygen functionalities. The well-formed beads of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite were employed to favor adsorptive capture and uptake of U(VI) from water due to the strong binding affinity of U(VI) ions to the loaded oxygen functional groups in the assembled composite. Multiple active functionalities, crystalline structure and average particle size (19.43 nm) were characterized. The EDX analysis referred to C: 47.89%, O: 44.77%, Zn: 0.43%, Ca: 2.82%, Cl: 1.74%, and Na: 2.23%. U(VI) adsorptive uptake was based on endothermic and spontaneous. Linear-nonlinear kinetic expressions confirmed high fitted to pseudo-second order kinetics with R2 0.99 and 0.94 for linear and nonlinear, respectively, while, the good agreement between the computed qe = 36.76 mg/g and qexp = 39.01 mg/g referred to the best fitting process by this model. The adsorptive uptake isotherms of U(VI) denoted mostly to good fitting with Langmuir adsorption isotherm in both linear and nonlinear representations. Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel as a novel nanohybrid composite was successfully implemented in removal of U(VI) from seawater, tap water and wastewater providing uptake percentages 93.52%, 90.51% and 92.68%, respectively. The evaluated composite was recycled 5 times and exhibited excellent effectiveness and high stability in U(VI) uptake with higher uptake capability when compared to other adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is a radioactive nuclide with excessive applications in atomic power reactors for energy generation without releasing hazardous gases as CO2 and CO which cause greenhouse phenomena. Furthermore, uranium is known to naturally exist in the ecosystem as pollutant in groundwater, rocks and soil and therefore, it is fundamentally released to water resources from the untreated wastewater [1]. The impact of uranium on the human body is related to the impulse by severe diseases as DNA deterioration, lung and kidney tumors, hereditary genetic disorder and hematological anomaly (leukemia). Moreover, uranium is cumulated in the central neural system cells. Subsequently, WHO and USEPA listed out the ultimate uranium concentration in fresh water with no more than 30 ppb [2]. Accordingly, more research efforts have been recently designed to find out safe and efficient strategies for potential implementation in removal of uranium ions from wastewater [3]. Uptake of uranium were reported by several processes including biological processing, solvent discharging, ion exchange, chemical sedimentation and chemical sorption in order to minimize the presence of uranium in water [2]. Among these approaches, adsorption was recently reported as the most commonly applied method because of its low price, high efficiency and operation simplicity [4]. Many components as inorganics, carbon-synthesized materials, polymers, MOFs (metal organic frameworks), magnetic compounds and bio-matrix compounds were utilized to synthesis various effective adsorbents [5]. The adsorption technique was advantageous to allow fabrication of highly selective adsorbents for uranium ions in presence of other coexisting cations as Mg, K, Na, Zn, Sr, Ca, etc. Corresponding to the hard and soft acid-base concept, uranium cation is listed as a Lewis acid which is able to create stabilized complexes with some functionalities including oxygen as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amidoxime and phosphate groups as Lewis bases [6]. Therefore, U(VI) ions were removed from aqueous matrices onto oxidized multiwalled carbon nanotubes with remarkable reusability [7]. The interaction mechanisms of uranium (VI) with chitosan hydrogel were explored based on the relationship between adsorbent and adsorbate [8]. Moreover, graphene oxide-mediated-Fe(II) was also used and studied to remediateU(VI) [9].

Carbon based materials as GO and its subordinates like rGO and modulated graphene oxide components are excellent adsorbents for U(VI) capture because of their 2D network assembly, surface reactive sites, physio-chemical distinguished properties, high surface area, strongly united connection of aromatic ring and its elevated thermal -mechanical stability [10]. It was found that graphene oxide and its derivatives could be applied to eliminate anionic uranium species at different pHs. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are luminescent carbon derived nanoparticles with incorporated visual and electronic characteristics. GQDs can be synthesized by carbonization of organic matters as citric acid. Furthermore, this material exhibited individual characteristics as declined toxicity, bio-convenient, high fluorescent interactivity, good photo-steadiness, firm chemical efficiency and surface-active sites as carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl to favor good chelating characteristics. Moreover, GQDs were reported as sensors for Hg2+ and ClO- recognition [11–13]. Carbon based materials are good candidates in adsorption process due to their structural properties and their modified forms were applied as adsorbents for removal of U(VI) using hydroxyapatite modifier [14]. Also magnetic graphene oxide with EDTA showed efficient removal process for U(VI) and decorated zero valent iron on rGO was also employed for the same purpose of U(VI) removal [15]. Adsorptive reduction of U(VI) onto rGO@zerovalent iron was also reported [16].

Metal organic frame works (MOFs) are classified as composed polymeric complexes of organic ligand unit like benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC), benzene dicarboxylic acid (BDC), and many others which are covalently bonded to some cations of transition metals, representative elements, actinides or may be combined with more than one metal ion. MOFs have been reported as good adsorbents for removal of U(VI) from aquatic matrices due to their high surface area, pore size, superior properties due to hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amino groups. Fe3O4 @AMCA-MIL53 magnetic MOFs was used for removal of U(VI) as well as for Th(VI) [17]. MIL-101 modified with amino- group was also studied for removing U(VI) by providing high sorption capacity [18]. Azo and amide groups were used for modification of MOFs to produce zinc azo MOF and this was applied in the process of U(VI) removal [19]. It has been also reported that zirconium terephthalate (UiO-66) modified with carbonylated graphene oxide as eliminator for uranium from sea water [20,21]. So, MOFs composites are labelled as excellent candidates in the purpose of U(VI) elimination of from water.

Poly-saccharide hydrogels are generally applied in wastewater treatment and purification due to their high removal efficiency, rapid elimination rate and recycling ability. On the other hand, hydrogels contain diverse obtainable sites like amide, carboxyl, sulfonic and hydroxyl groups to enable elimination and capture of different cations from polluted water. Sodium alginate, which is extracted from nautical brown algae is composed of many compounds as L-guluronic acid and 1,4-bonded d-mannuronic acid. Alginate may be constructed into three-dimensional hydrogel beads when interlinking with aqueous calcium cation. It was reported that calcium bonded alginate hydrogel was highly effective for elimination of heavy metals from water. However, conventional alginate hydrogel has disadvantages as unsteady physical combination that prohibit their utilizing in a complicated medium as it enhances degeneration and gelation operation [22–24] . Modification of natural polymers with organic or inorganic component was identified to achieve good removal capacity as their synthesized forms not only relying on chemical characteristics of their compounds but also to the collaboration between them [23]. Despite the wide spread of using hydrogels as modifiers for removal of pollutants as metal ions, few research articles were reported for removal of U(VI) using hydrogels as modifiers. For example, amidoxime hydrogel was reported for extraction of U(VI) from aquatic environment [25]. Due to the ability of U(VI) to strongly bind with oxygenated functionalities, this work has been designed to generate a novel and well-formed beads of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite from the combination of zinc MOF using benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC) as a ligand with two different carbon based materials, GQDs and rGO and these were aimed to interlink via a network of alginate hydrogel to achieve the incorporation of a variety of oxygenated functional groups in the assembled hydrogel composite to establish and enhance higher adsorptive capture and elimination of U(VI) ions from aquatic matrices.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2, FW 136.28 g/mol, 97%), U(VI) acetate (UO₂(CH₃CO₂)2·H₂O, FW 424.15 g/mol, and 98%), sodium alginate (C6H9NaO7, 98%) benzene tricarboxylic acid (H3BTC, FW 207.12 g/mol, and 95%) and NH2NH2·H2O (FW 50.06 g/mol, and ≥98%) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Na2CO3 (FW 105.98 g/mol, and 99.88%), KCl (FW 74.55 g/mol, and 99%), NaOH (FW 40 g/mol, and 98%), NaNO3 (FW 84.99 g/mol, and 98%), HCl (FW 36.46 g/mol, and 37%) and graphite powder were acquired from WINLAB Ltd. chemicals company·H2O2 (FW 34.01 g/mol, and 30%), CaCl2 (FW 110.98 g/mol, and 99%), H2SO4 (FW 98.08 g/mol, and 98%), KMnO4 (FW 158.04 g/mol, and 99%), H3PO4 (FW 97.99 g/mol, and 85%), L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, FW 176.12 g/mol, and 30%), N,N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, FW 73.09 g/mol, and 99.8%), NaCl (FW 58.44 g/mol, and 99%), and citric acid anhydrous (C₆H₈O₇, FW 192.12 g/mol, and 99.5%) were acquired from VWR international Ltd, Poole, BH151 TD, England.

U(VI) absorbance was measured by a UV–visible 2700 adsorption spectrophotometer. The shaking operation was applied by automatic shaker at speed 150 rpm. Adwa pH-meter standardized using different buffer solutions was utilized to adjust U(VI) pH values. BRUKER VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrophotometer at 400–4500 cm−1 was used to measure the FTIR spectra of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid composite. JEOL-JFC-1100E was applied to obtain scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. JEOL-JSM-1400Plus, Japan transmission electron microscopy was utilized to measure the image. TGA-50-Schimadzu, Japan was utilized to obtain thermal analysis and the X-ray diffraction pattern was obtained by XRD Shimadzu lab, Japan.

GO was manufactured by modulated Hummer’s process. Briefly, 5.0 g of graphite powder was combined with 2.5 g NaNO3, 108 mL of H2SO4 and 12 mL H3PO4 in 1000 mL beaker then the blend was stirred in an ice bath for 10 min. Moreover, 15.0 g of KMnO4 was gradually added to the blend keeping the temperature lower than 5ᴏC with constant stirring in the ice bath for 1 h. Then, the blend was stirred for 1 h at 40 ᴏC. Thereafter, the blend was stirred for 1 h at 98 ᴏC, after that double distilled water (DDW) was slowly added till the volume reached to 400 mL, to which 20 mL of H2O2 was added. Then the formed GO particles were centrifuged and washed several times with 5% HCl, then with DDW till neutralization. Finally GO particles were dried in an oven at 70 °C for 12 h [21].

4.0 g of (GO) was added to 100 mL of DDW in a 500 mL beaker and stirred for 30 min, while the pH was adjusted to 10 using 0.1 M NaOH and stirred for 30 min. Finally, 80 mL of hydrazine monohydrate was added to the mixture and refluxed for 8 h. The resulting rGO was washed with DDW several times and dried at 70 °C in an oven for 12 h [26,27].

In a 50 mL beaker, 2.0 g of citric acid was added and heated at 200 ᴏC for 30 min. At the first 5 min of heating, the citric acid turned to yellow color then it was gradually transformed to dark orange after 30 min. Finally, the carbonized citric acid was added to 10 g/L NaOH and stirred well, then the mixture was dried at 70 ᴏC in an oven. This batch was repeated three times [28,29].

In a 250 mL beaker, 0.84 g of H3BTC was added to 1.0889 g of zinc chloride followed by 20 mL N,N dimethylformamide and stirred well for 20 min. Then the mixture was combined with a 1.0 g sample of rGO, 1.0 g GQDs and 20 mL N,N-dimethylformamide. This mixture was stirred for 1 h and finally, the pH was adjusted to 9 using triethylamine and refluxed for 12 h at 75 ᴏC [30,31].

In a 500 mL beaker, 3.0 g of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO was added to 300 mL distilled water and 3.0 g sodium alginate was gradually added with continuous stirring till homogenous mixture was formed. Then, using 10 mL syringe the bead of hydrogel is formed in petri dishes each contain 20 mL of 0.5 M CaCl2 crosslinker as the formed beads was left in CaCl2 for 24 h. Eventually, the beads were washed with water several times and left to dry in an oven at 60 ᴏC [32].

1000 mg L−1 stock solution of U(VI) was prepared and converted to 50 and 100 mg L−1 solutions. The absorbance was measured at λmax = 360 nm by UV spectroscopy device. The chromogenic reagent of U(VI) was prepared by adding 70 mL of 1.0 M of L-ascorbic acid, 40 mL of 0.5 M sodium carbonate and 90 mL of distilled water. The capture processes were applied at normal room temperature and the interactions were applied by automatic shaker. The capture of U(VI) by the nanohybrid composite was carried out under several practical specifications as the impact of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel dose, contact time, ionic strength, concentration of U(VI), pH, and temperature [33].

U(VI) solution was regulated at pH domain from 2.4 to 9.6 of concentrations 50 and 100 mg L− 1 by 0.1 M of NaOH and HCl. 10 mL of U(VI) in different pH solutions were shaken with 20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel for 30 min and followed by filtration, while the remaining U(VI) ions were estimated by a UV–visible spectrophotometer at λmax = 360 nm.

0.1 g of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite was added to 40 mL of 0.01 M KCl and the pH of solutions was controlled in the extent from 2 to 11 by either 0.1 M of NaOH or HCl then the blend was shaken by automatic shaker for 4 h. The final pH was determined after 12 h and the PZC was determined by plotting the graph between ΔpH (pHfinal-pHinitial) versus pH.

Different masses of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite in the extent from 5 mg to 100 mg were shaken with 10 mL of U(VI) (50 and 100 mg L−1 concentrations) at pH 7, followed by filtration and the remaining U(VI) were estimated.

Diverse shaking times (2–60 min) by using automatic shaker were applied. 20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite was mixed with 10 mL of 50 or 100 mg L−1 concentration of U(VI) and the pH of solutions was regulated at 7. After refining, the absorbance magnitudes of residual quantities of U(VI) were estimated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of initial concentration of U(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL of 50 mg L−1 to 100 mg L−1 U(VI) were shaken with 20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite at pH 7 for 30 min, then filtered and finally the concentration of the remaining U(VI) was determined.

Different doses of NaCl in the domain from 10 mg to 100 mg were added to 20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite, shaken with 10 mL of 50 and 100 mg L−1 of U(VI) at pH 7 for 30 min, then filtered and finally the concentration of the remaining U(VI) was then determined.

20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite was shaken with 10 mL of 50 and 100 mg L−1 U(VI) at pH 7 for 30 min at various temperatures domain from 25 °C to 60 °C, then filtered and finally the remaining U(VI) concentration was determined.

Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite was added to 50 mg L−1 U(VI) and shaken for 30 min, then after, washed with 100 mL of 0.1 M HCl and DDW till the pH reached to 7. Moreover, the resulted composite was dried in an oven at 70 ᴏC and reused as 20 mg of the composite was then added to 10 mL of 50 mg L−1 U(VI) solution and shaken for 30 min. It was then filtered and finally, the concentration of the remaining U(VI) was determined. This process was repeated for five recycling times.

The practical implementation of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite in the adsorptive capture of U(VI) from actual water matrices (tap water-sea water-industrial wastewater) was conducted and applied by using spiked samples with 50 and 100 mg L−1 U(VI). 10 mL of various U(VI) solutions were combined with 20 mg of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel composite at pH 7 and the blends were shaken for 30 min. Ultimately, the blends were refined and the absorbance magnitudes were determined as reported above.

FTIR analysis is used to interpret the surface functionality. For GO, the illustrated FTIR in Fig. 1a exhibits several peaks at 3404 cm−1 for the hydroxyl stretching of carboxylic acid, 1717 cm−1 for the carbonyl group, 1236 cm−1 for the alcohol group (C-OH), 1055 cm−1 for C-O-C and 1583 cm−1 for the SP2 hybridized CC in-plane vibration. The existence of such oxygenated groups refer to the successful oxidation process [34,35]. rGO was characterized by a decrease in the consistency of peaks in contrast to FTIR of GO due to the reduction process. The peak at 3436 cm−1 appeared with a lower intensity, while the carbonyl band at 1717 cm−1 almost disappeared and C-OH band at 1236 cm−1 was disappeared. Furthermore, the bands at 1574 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of CC of phenolic ring, 1412 cm−1 to C-H bending vibration and 1109 cm−1 to the C-O epoxide stretching [34]. The prepared GQDs exhibited some peaks at 3469 cm−1 and 2700 cm−1 due to stretching vibration of hydroxyl group for coordinated and coordinated water molecules, while the band at 1654 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibration of carbonyl group, while the bands 1561 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 confirming the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate group [11,24]. Zn-BTC MOF exhibited a band at 3426 cm−1 for the stretching vibration of –OH in carboxylate group, the bands located at 1628 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1 as well as 1440 cm−1 and 1369 cm−1 confirm the asymmetric and symmetric carbonyl stretching vibrations, respectively. Due to the coordination of H3BTC ligand with zinc metal, the band appeared at 466 cm−1 is corresponding to Zn-O bond to refer to a successful fabrication of Zn-BTC MOF [36–38]. Finally the FTIR of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid showed a band at 3426 cm−1 for the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group of carboxylate group, while the band at 1627 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibration of carbonyl group, 1430 cm−1 confirming the bending vibration of carboxylate as the distinctive peak of sodium alginate. The peaks appeared at 1082 cm−1,1027 cm−1 are corresponding to C-O-C stretching vibration [38,39] .

The XRD of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid exhibited several diffraction angles at 2θ = 8.84 ᴏ, 10.10 ᴏ,18.24 and 23.6 ᴏ for Zn-BTC which are well matched with CCDC number 1274034 [40,41]. The diffraction 2θ peaks at approximately 25ᴏ and 45ᴏ showed as the Bragg’s scattering angles indicate primary undoped rGO plates according to JCPDS Card No. 41–1487 and the slight broadness in peak reflect less crystallinity as illustrated in Fig.1b [42,43].

As shown in Fig. 1c, the XPS spectra is utilized to indicate the strength of scattered photo electrons from several atoms. The survey of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid confirms the surface constituents are C 1 s with atomic percentage 55.38%, O 1 s = 25.85%, Ca 2p = 3.53%, Zn 2p = 1.57%, Cl 2p = 6.58%, Na 1 s = 4.8% and N 1 s = 2.29%. The C 1 s peak at 284.21 eV refers to C- sp2 hybridization [44], the peak at 285.7 eV indicates C-O [45], while the peak at 286 eV confirms C-OH and the peak at 287.9 eV shows carboxylate group [46]. The O1s peaks at 532 eV and 533 eV indicate C-OH [45]. The peaks at 1021.95,1023.36 eV are for bonded divalent zinc with oxygen and the bands at 1045.02,1046.23 eV confirm the presence of zinc [47]. The bands of Ca 2p are conformable to the primary peak of Ca 2p3/2 (347.76 eV) and the secondary band of Ca 2p1/2 (351.22 eV) [46]. The occurrence of calcium and chloride ions owing to hydrogel cross-linker are the Cl 2p band at 201.3 eV and Ca 2p band at 349.3 eV. The peak for N 1 s at 400 eV is due to the remaining hydrazine monohydrate reducing agent for GO [48]. Traces of sodium are also present at 1073.32 eV showed in Fig. 1c.

Thermal stability of the prepared nanohybrid is illustrated in Fig. 1d. The weight loss at 111.59 ᴏC is 0.1724 mg with 9.579% loss attributed to water evaporation from the adsorbent surface and the pyrolysis of scarce particles present in the hydrogel. At 222.49ᴏC, the weight loss is 0.1019 mg with 5.664% as the oxygen including groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl in rGO, GQD and alginate hydrogel started to decay to CO, CO2, and steam [39,49]. Moreover, the liberation of water particles from the pores of Zn-BTC@ MOF was happened at slightly higher temperature than 200 ᴏC. High weight loss 0.3792 mg with 21.07% was observed at 331.10 ᴏC due to continued decay of oxygenated groups and Zn-BTC MOF decomposed to ZnO [39,40] [25,34]. At temperatures 480.82ᴏC and 583.12 ᴏC the weight losses were 0.1669 mg with 9. 277% and 0.06173 mg with 3.430% due to the pyrolysis of the shell of carbon in rGO [49].

The morphology of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was determined by SEM and HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1e. The SEM of as-prepared composite shows aggregation of the particles and that may be attributed to the hydrogel formation which links all the particles together. Also, the image refers to the presence of micropores due to the presence of microporous MOF and alginate structures which favor and enhance the adsorption and uptake process of U(VI) ions [21]. Regarding HR-TEM image, it has been illustrated that the particles show uniform distribution with demonstrated average particle size 19.43 nm as shown in Fig. 1 f [24,49].

The EDX spectrum is known as a quantitative analysis for determining the adsorbent elemental percentage as carbon atomic percentage = 47.89%, Oxygen = 44.77%, Zn = 0.43%, Ca = 2.82%, Cl = 1.74%, Si = 0.11% and Na = 2.23% to indicate that the Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was successfully fabricated as illustrated in Fig. 1 g [21,24,49] .

U(VI) removal from water by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was determined by the batch method via measuring the (%E) values of U(VI) which are revealed by the absorbance of the U(VI) before and after uptake [50] as mentioned in the following Eq. (1).

The magnitude of U(VI) uptake at equilibrium was evaluated by the following Eq. (2).

Where the initial and final U(VI) concentrations are Co and Ce, respectively. V(L) is the volume and W(g) is the mass.

The pH range from 2.4 to 9.59 was applied to evaluate the effectiveness of pH on the elimination capacity of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. At pH 2, the elimination capacity was very low providing 25.66% and 34.27% for 100 mg L−1 and 50 mg L−1 concentrations, respectively. Below pH 4, uranium ions are present in the forms of UO2 2+, (UO2)4(OH)7 + and UO2OH+. In addition, the adsorbent surface in presence of pH 2 is positively charged, so poor elimination capacity at acidic pH was detected as illustrated in Fig. 2a. With increasing the solution pH, the uptake was reached to optimum at pH 7.12 with removal efficiency corresponding to 68.85% and 79.49% for 100 mg L−1 and 50 mg L−1 concentrations due to the dominant uranium UO2CO3 and UO2(CO3)2 2- forms with slight presence of UO2OH+ (UO2)2(OH)2 + forms to indicate that Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid could coordinate with these structures due to the presence of oxygen containing functional groups. Also, at higher pH the carboxylate group of the alginate as well as oxygen function groups in alginate as well as other varieties of oxygenated functional groups in GQDs and MOFs which are good candidates for complexation and ion pair formation [52–55]. With increasing the pH value to pH 9.59, the uptake capacities were declined once again to reach to 40.17% and 48.67% due to the presence of more hydrolyzed and dominant (UO2)3(OH)10 4-, (UO2) (CO3) (OH)3 4-, UO2(CO3)2 2-, UO2(CO3)4- and UO2(CO3)3 4- as UO2(CO3)3 4- forms which are difficult to bind and complex with negatively charged surface of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid [20,51].

Finally, the PZC of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was detected and characterized at pH 6 to reveal that at this point the adsorbent total charge is zero as illustrated in Fig. 2b. Below pH 6 the superficies charge is generally positive and above pH 6, the superficies charge is generally negative. The value of PZC for Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid can be used to describe the uptake performance of this composite at various pH conditions as previously described [56].

The dosage of adsorbent is an important factor in the uptake of U(VI) by the applicable composite. For this purpose, a broad range of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid masses from 5 to 50 mg was examined to detect the effectiveness of this composite in adsorptive uptake of U(VI). As illustrated in Fig. 3, upon rising the mass of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg nanohybrid, the percentage of removal of U(VI) was elevated to confirm that the number of active sites on the adsorbent exterior were high enough until attaining the equilibrium state. The highest removal percentages were achievedas 67.74% and 78.83% using 30 mg mass of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid utilizing 100 and 50 mg L−1 U(VI) concentrations, respectively [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of contact time and kinetic studies
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reaction time on the uptake removal of U(VI) onto Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was studied with a large domain of time from 2 min to 60 min. It was observed that with elevating the time, the uptake removal was progressively enhanced till attaining the equilibrium state at 30 min. At the first step, the empty active positions on the Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid surface were gradually occupied by U(VI) ions and at the equilibrium state all the active sites were completely saturated by the adsorbed U(VI) ion and therefore, no more increase in the uptake removal was detected. As stated in Fig. 4a, the U(VI) concentrations 100 and 50 mg L−1 were found to record the following removal percentage 34.21% and 41. 3% after 2 min. However at optimum time (30 min), the removal percentages reached the highest values to read 69.74% and 75.36% by 100 and 50 mg L−1, respectively [58,59].

The linear and nonlinear kinetic frameworks were utilized to determine the removal interaction mechanism of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid via application of several kinetic frameworks to characterize the best fitting framework. The evaluated kinetic frameworks (theoretical and experimental) are counting on the special correlation coefficient R2 value. Commonly, high R2 value reveals that the kinetic framework is fitting to describe the uptake removal reaction of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. The definition and parameters of pseudo-first order [60] framework are mentioned in Table 1. The plots in Fig. 4b resulted in R2 values = 0.9477 and 0.9166 for 100 and 50 mg L−1 U(VI), respectively. Also, the calculated (qe cal) and experimental (qe exp) magnitudes were not close as stated in Table 2. Thus, pseudo-first order is not representing the best fitting to describe the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid.

Then pseudo-second order (PSO) framework [61] was applied as shown in Fig. 4c. When t/qt was plotted against time, the slope and intercept were corresponded to 1⁄qe and 1⁄k2qe 2, respectively. The computed R2 values = 0.9609 and 0.9598 for 100 and 50 mg L−1 concentrations U(VI), respectively. The PSO framework was applied to identify that the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid (qe cal) was highly fitting with t(qexp) as stated in Table 2 [61]. The intraparticle diffusion framework assumes that the uptake removal of U(VI) may rely on two fundamental strides. The first is based on the transfer of U(VI) ions from the solution to the exterior of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid, and the second is the prevalence of U(VI) into the Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid pores [57]. According to this framework, the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid depends on t1/2 rather than t. The graph in Fig. 4d shows kid as the slope, beside C as the intercept. The R2 of 100 and 50 mg L−1 U(VI) were 0.9707 and 0.9276, respectively [57]. The Elovich framework is fitting with arrangement to exhibit heterogeneous surface and it is also convenient to describe chemisorption interactions [62]. As showed in Fig. 4e, when the graph stated a straight line resulted with 1/β as slope and 1/β ln(βα) as intercept. The calculated R2 were 0.8804 and 0.8204 for U(VI) 100 and 50 mg L−1 respectively.

According to the computed and listed R2 values in Table 2 by the four investigated frameworks, it is evident that the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was best fitting to the pseudo-second order framework and the removal process was mainly related to a chemisorption mechanism as previously reported in other studies [63,64]. For better evaluation of the kinetic studies, nonlinear pseudo-first order, pseudo-second order, intraparticle diffusion and Elovich expressions were studied as illustrated in Fig. (4 f, 4 g) and the characterized parameters were also illustrated in Table 2. It was confirmed from this investigation that the nonlinear kinetic studies are in accordance with the linear ones as well, confirming the better fitting of the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid to the pseudo second order model [63–65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of U(VI) concentrations and uptake isotherms
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of U(VI) concentration ranging from 50 to 100 mg L−1 on the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was also studied. The percentage removal was descended with promotion of the U(VI) concentration ( Fig. 5a) due to the deficiency in functional groups. In spite of descending the removal capacity, the amount of removed U(VI) per mass was enhanced by the rising in U(VI) concentration because of the growing U(VI) mobile power that increased the extending of the U(VI) onto the surface of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid that manipulate the mass transfer of U(VI) from the solution to the Zn-BTC@ /GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid at high concentration of the U(VI) [66].

Linear and nonlinear isotherm frameworks including Langmuir, Freundlich, Dubinin–Rasushkevich and Temkin ( Table 3) were studied to determine the possible interaction mechanism(s) for the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. For linear adsorption isotherm, Langmuir framework was applied to count for a monolayer adsorption reaction on a uniform superficies with no conjunction between the removed ions [67]. As shown in Fig. 5b, high R2 0.9995 affirms the fitting of Langmuir pattern to depict the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. The slope and the intercept were concluded for evaluation of qmax and b ( Table 4). The RL (separation coefficient) is utilized to detect the kind of removal process (Table 3). If 0 < RL< 1, it provides refers to a favorable technique, if RL = 0, it indicates an irreversible technique, while the RL > 1 is used for unfavorable process and if RL = 1, it confirms a linear technique. The identified RL magnitudes were 0.193–0.075 which approve a favorable uptake removal process of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid [67].

The Freundlich framework presumed the formation of multilayer uptake process to take place on heterogeneous superficies which implicate dissimilar active sites with several adsorption energies (Table 3). As shown in Fig. 5c, the computed R2 was 0.9897 as listed in Table 4 [68]. The Temkin framework hypothesized that the heat of uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid could directly decrease with the elevation of the adsorbent superficies encasement as the incorporation between adsorbent and U(VI). Fig. 5d refers to R2 of U(VI) uptake by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid determined as 0.9974 [69,70]. The Dubinin–Rasushkevich (D-R) framework is utilized to interpret the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid as well as the porosity and the uptake energy (Es) (Table 3). The plot expressed in Fig. 5e resulted in a straight line with R2 estimates corresponding to 0.9367. The computed Es > 16 (kJ mol−1) refers to a chemisorption, while the Es < 8 (kJ mol−1) points out to physisorption [71]. According to the collected data (Table 4), the Es estimate was 0.25 kJ mol−1 for uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid to reveal that the uptake technique is physisorption. Therefore, it is clear that the Langmuir isotherm is the most convenient model to explicate the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid [72].

Finally, the nonlinear adsorption isotherm by the same four models were also applied as illustrated in Fig. 5 f and their parameters also compiled in Table 5. In the nonlinear Langmuir isotherm, the computed R2 = 0.9979 with maximum capacity 35.85 mg/g, while the nonlinear Freundlich isotherm showed a correlation factor R2 = 0.98921 with n = 0.29026. Regarding the nonlinear Temkin isotherm, R2 = 0.99744 with elevated bT of 310.34517 j/mol were identified to refer to strong interaction with the pollutants. The nonlinear D-R isotherm exihibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 31.03795 and R2 = 0.93354 as mentioned in Table 5. From the correlation factors of both linear and nonlinear adsorption isotherm it is concluded that the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was mainly following and proceeding according to Langmuir adsorption isotherm [24].

Various extents of NaCl salt (10–100 mg) were investigated to detect the impact of ionic strength concentration on the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid as shown in Fig. 6. It is evident that 10 mg of NaCl salt provided low decline in the U(VI) uptake with percentage 68.84% and 80.36% for 100 and 50 mg L−1, respectively. By increasing the salt to 100 mg, the uptake percentage of U(VI) were also declined to 45.6% and 54.76% as represented in Fig. 6. The decrease in uptake removal performance of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid may be contributed by the elevation in the salt concentration which efficiently compete to the same active sites of adsorbent exterior [56].

The contribution of reaction temperature from 25 °C to 60 °C on the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was investigated to detect the uptake values by using 50 and 100 mg L−1 concentrations of U(VI) and the collected results are shown in Fig. 7a. Endothermic uptake of U(VI) was obtained due to the increase in the uptake of U(VI) with increasing the reaction temperature. A gradual increase was mainly related to the evolving of the binding rate between U(VI) and Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid superficies [73]. The thermodynamics for the uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid were also computed based on (ΔS°, ΔH° and ΔG°) free energy according to Eqs. (3–5).
(3) ΔG° = -RT lnKD

Where T is the temperature (K), KD(L/g) represents the equilibrium constant and R(J/mol.k) is the general gas constant, while KD can be calculated from Eqs. (4 and 5).
(4) KD=qe/ce
(5) ln KD = ΔS°/R - ΔH°/RT

As Ce (mg/L) refers to concentration of U(VI) at equilibrium, qe denotes to the U(VI) adsorbed by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid (mg/g). From the linear relation between lnKD and 1/T, the slope and intercept could be concluded and employed for calculation of ΔH° and ΔS°, respectively (Fig. 7b). The outlined ΔG° in Table 6 show a negative value indicating a spontaneous uptake removal of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. Furthermore, the positive values of (ΔS°) refers to increasing the randomness, while the detected positive value of (ΔH°) confirms the endothermic uptake interaction [57].

The loaded Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid with adsorbed U(VI) ions was collected and washed with 100 mL of 0.1 M HCl to release U(VI) ions from the surface. The recycled adsorbent was repeatedly washed with distilled water till the medium become neutral (pH 7). The uptake removal of U(VI) by recycled Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid was further studied and the results are listed in Table 7. It evident that a slight decrease in the uptake percentage to produce 75.29% after the first cycle. The recycling process was then repeated for 4 more cycles as given in Table 7 to explore the removal efficiency recycled Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. It can be concluded that the investigated Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid proved high uptake behavior due to possible regeneratation and utilization for 5 cycles with limited decrease in the uptake effectiveness [21].

Several water specimens were collected and applied to examine the uptake performance of U(VI) by Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid. The spiked 50 mg L−1 concentration of U(VI) was found to refer to the following uptake percentage values 93.52%, 90.51% and 92.68% from tap water, seawater, and wastewater, respectively. Also, the spiked 100 mg L−1 concentration recorded the following U(VI) uptake percentage values 91.19%, 90.78% and 91.53% from tap water, sea water and wastewater specimens, respectively. Moreover, the above mentioned results assured that Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid is convenient as efficient adsorbent for removal and uptake of U(VI) from actual waters with high capacity value as outlined in Table 8 versus other reported adsorbents [74–83].

The investigated Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid composite was fabricated from zinc ions with trimesic acid, GQDs, rGO, sodium alginate hydrogel. The assembled Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid exhibited excellent uptake effectiveness of U(VI) from aqueous solution and other water samples as seawater, tap-water and wastewater providing 93.52%, 90.51% and 92.68% recovery from spiked 50 mg L−1 concentration of U(VI). The analysis of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel by FTIR indicated the presence of several functionalities as -CO, -COOH and –OH. XRD. The Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel reactivity was checked by alternative factors to estimate the optimum conditions for the U(VI) uptake and these were identified as 20 mg mass, 30 min mobilization time and pH 7. The consequence of temperature on the uptake of U(VI) and thermodynamic parameters referred to endothermic and spontaneous interaction. The limitation of the prepared Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel nanohybrid is related to the cost-effectiveness of trimesic acid used in the preparation step of MOF. As illustrated in Table 8, a comparison between the prepared nanohybrid hydrogel in this work versus other adsorbents indicated the superiority of Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel [74–83]. Moreover, the exploed Zn-BTC@GQDs-rGO@Alg hydrogel may be used for removal of other radionucleotides in the future.

Mohamed E. Mahmoud: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Rana M. Tharwat: Data curation, Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Amir M. Abdelfattah: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Saad S.M. Hassan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
